title: Protein Folding Disorders
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: Richard I. Morimoto, G. Scott Budinger

items.subtopic: Protein Folding Disorders
items.title: Understanding Protein Misfolding Diseases
items.content: Protein misfolding disorders arise from errors in protein folding, influenced by aging and environmental stress. These disorders affect cellular health and longevity by disrupting proteostasis.
keyPoints: Protein misfolding can lead to diseases like Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, ALS, and Huntington's disease.
Aging is a major risk factor for these protein folding disorders.
The proteostasis network (PN) maintains cellular health by managing protein synthesis, folding, and degradation.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · Protein Folding Disorders

items.subtopic: Protein Folding Disorders
items.title: Role of the Proteostasis Network in Health
items.content: The PN is crucial for maintaining a stable proteome throughout life. It involves molecular chaperones that ensure protein functionality and prevent aggregation.
keyPoints: PN consists of ~3000 molecular chaperones essential for protein management.
It balances protein synthesis, folding, translocation, and degradation.
Dysfunction in PN can lead to age-associated diseases and increased morbidity.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · Protein Folding Disorders

items.subtopic: Protein Folding Disorders
items.title: Impact of Genetic Mutations and Environmental Stressors
items.content: Genetic mutations and environmental stressors challenge the PN, leading to protein misfolding and cellular dysfunction.
keyPoints: Genetic mutations add complexity to the PN's role in maintaining proteostasis.
Environmental stressors can overwhelm the PN, causing disease manifestations.
Clinicians observe proteostasis failure in age-related disorders and acute illnesses.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · Protein Folding Disorders

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)
items.title: Role of Protein Quality Control in Managing Folding and Misfolding
items.content: The protein network (PN) is crucial for managing the synthesis, folding, and degradation of proteins to prevent aggregation-prone intermediates. It employs mechanisms like selective disaggregation or degradation to handle off-pathway aggregates.
keyPoints: PN ensures functional folding and minimizes accumulation of aggregation-prone intermediates.
Built-in redundancy allows tolerance for chemical noise and sequence diversity from polymorphisms and biosynthetic errors.
Proteins are sensitive to intracellular environment changes like pH, energetics, and external stress conditions.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)
items.title: Chaperone Function in Protein Folding
items.content: Chaperones such as HSP70, HSP90, and others are integral to co- and posttranslational folding. They regulate protein states through ATP binding cycles and interact with partially folded intermediates.
keyPoints: Approximately 60% of the proteome is regulated by chaperones in the cytoplasm and nucleus.
HSP70 and J-domain family chaperones use nucleotide hydrolysis to manage folding states.
HSP90 regulates folding for specific proteins like transcription factors, kinases, and signaling molecules.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)
items.title: Mitochondrial and ER Protein Quality Control
items.content: Chaperones are essential for mitochondrial-targeted proteins to maintain recognition-competent states for translocation. The ER uses chaperones like glycosyltransferases and calnexins for protein folding.
keyPoints: Mitochondria account for ~20% of the proteome, with most proteins encoded by the nuclear genome.
Chaperone interactions are crucial for mitochondrial protein import and folding.
ER chaperones manage misfolded proteins through retro-translocation to the cytoplasm for degradation.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 2)
items.title: Role of UPS and ALP in Protein Degradation
items.content: The ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS) is the primary pathway for protein degradation, tagging proteins with ubiquitin via a cascade involving E1, E2, and E3 enzymes. The autophagy-lysosomal pathway (ALP) responds to nutrient deprivation and damage by degrading large aggregates and organelles.
keyPoints: UPS is the primary system for protein degradation.
Involves enzymatic cascade with E1, E2, and E3 enzymes.
~750 ubiquitin E3 ligases contribute to client specificity.
ALP responds to nutrient deprivation and damage.
Chaperones can recruit proteins for proteasomal or lysosomal degradation.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 2)
items.title: Function of Ubiquitination in Protein Quality Control
items.content: Ubiquitination is crucial for protein quality control, marking proteins for degradation and preventing aggregation. It involves E3 ligases like listerin and CHIP, which help decide between client folding or proteasome-mediated degradation.
keyPoints: Ubiquitination marks proteins for degradation to prevent aggregation.
Listerin ubiquitinates stalled nascent chains during protein synthesis.
CHIP ubiquitinates nonnative aggregates in the HSP70/HSP90 complex.
ERAD involves retro-translocation and cytosolic proteasomal degradation of misfolded ER-targeted proteins.
Ubiquitination links proteasome and autophagy pathways for lysosomal degradation.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)
items.title: HSR Activation and Regulation by HSF-1
items.content: The heat shock response (HSR) is a critical cellular defense mechanism against proteotoxic stress, regulated by HSF-1. Upon exposure to stressors like heat shock, HSF-1 trimerizes, undergoes posttranslational modifications, and activates transcription of genes encoding molecular chaperones.
keyPoints: HSF-1 exists in an inert monomeric state under unstressed conditions.
Upon stress, HSF-1 trimerizes, acquires DNA-binding activity, and forms nuclear stress bodies.
Posttranslational modifications of HSF-1 include phosphorylation and sumoylation.
Repression of HSF-1 involves acetylation and binding with chaperones like HSP70, leading to its dissociation back to the monomeric state.
HSF-1 is essential for stress resilience, early development in metazoans, gametogenesis, oocyte maturation, cancer progression, and maintaining NAD⁺ and ATP levels.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)
items.title: ER Stress Response via UPR$^{ER}$
items.content: The unfolded protein response of the endoplasmic reticulum (UPR$^{ER}$) is mediated by three transcription factors: XBP1, ATF6, and ATF4. These pathways are activated in response to misfolded proteins in the ER.
keyPoints: XBP1 activation involves IRE1's RNase activity that splices XBP1 mRNA into a form (XBP1s) inducing UPR target genes.
ATF6 is relocalized from the ER membrane to the Golgi, where it is cleaved and translocates to the nucleus to activate transcription of UPR genes.
PERK phosphorylates eIF2α linking UPR$^{ER}$ with the integrated stress response (ISR).
Together, these pathways regulate protein folding, degradation, and lipid metabolism.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)
items.title: Mitochondrial Stress Response via UPR$^{mt}$
items.content: The mitochondrial unfolded protein response (UPR$^{mt}$) is regulated by ATFS1 and its mammalian orthologue ATF5. This pathway responds to mitochondrial stress by regulating transcription of genes involved in mitochondrial function.
keyPoints: Under normal conditions, ATFS1 is imported into mitochondria and degraded; under stress, it translocates to the nucleus.
ATF5 regulates UPR$^{mt}$ in mammals, similar to ATFS1's role in C. elegans.
UPR$^{mt}$ induces genes for mitochondrial chaperones, import machinery, and glycolysis.
Mitochondrial electron transport inhibitors activate the ISR via OMA1 cleaving DELE1 to activate HRI.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 2)
items.title: How ISR Kinases Regulate Protein Synthesis Under Stress
items.content: The Integrated Stress Response (ISR) is activated by kinases PKR, PERK, HRI, and GCN2, which phosphorylate eIF2a to globally inhibit protein synthesis. This paradoxically enhances translation of specific mRNAs like ATF4, promoting stress resilience while also potentially lowering the threshold for apoptosis through CHOP.
keyPoints: ISR is induced by PKR, PERK, HRI, or GCN2 kinases.
These kinases phosphorylate eIF2a to regulate protein synthesis.
Global inhibition of protein synthesis paradoxically promotes translation of specific mRNAs like ATF4.
ATF4 induces genes for stress resilience and lowers apoptosis threshold via CHOP.
Activation of ISR can impair stem cell differentiation, linking mitochondrial dysfunction with aging.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 2)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 2)
items.title: Role of NRF2 in Oxidative Stress Response
items.content: NRF2, regulated by the ubiquitin ligase KEAP-1, is stabilized and translocated to the nucleus under oxidative stress. This leads to the induction of antioxidant proteins and detoxification enzymes, highlighting its critical role in managing oxidative and xenobiotic stresses.
keyPoints: Oxidative stress activates NRF2 by inhibiting its negative regulator KEAP-1.
NRF2 translocates to the nucleus under stress conditions.
Induces expression of antioxidant proteins and detoxification enzymes.
Corresponds to SKN-1 in C. elegans, integrating redox-regulatory pathways.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Hormesis in Protein Quality Control
items.content: Chronic low-level activation of the Heat Shock Response (HSR), Unfolded Protein Response in the Endoplasmic Reticulum (UPR^ER), and Mitochondrial UPR (UPR^mt) is protective against extreme stress conditions.
keyPoints: Hormesis involves chronic low-level activation of HSR, UPR^ER, and UPR^mt.
These pathways are protective against lethal cell stress.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Neuronal Regulation of Stress Responses
items.content: In C. elegans, neuronal signaling regulates the HSR, UPR^ER, and UPR^mt at an organismal level, with disruptions in these pathways reverting to cell-autonomous control.
keyPoints: HSR, UPR^ER, and UPR^mt are regulated by sensory neurons in C. elegans.
Impaired neuronal signaling causes reversion to cell-autonomous control.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Mitochondrial Dysfunction and Longevity
items.content: Mild perturbation of the mitochondrial electron transfer chain (ETC) increases lifespan by activating UPR^mt, with systemic benefits mediated through pathways like insulin signaling.
keyPoints: Perturbation of ETC increases lifespan in invertebrates and rodents.
Activation of UPR^mt is involved in stress resilience and longevity.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 2) | PROPERTIES OF PROTEIN FOLDING DISEASES
items.title: Proteostasis Decline and Aging-Related Diseases
items.content: The decline in proteostasis is a key hallmark of aging, contributing to the development of numerous degenerative diseases associated with protein misfolding. As proteostasis deteriorates, it impacts other pillars of geroscience, potentially leading to widespread tissue dysfunction.
keyPoints: Proteostasis is one of the primary hallmarks of aging.
Failure in protein quality control negatively affects other aspects of aging biology.
Proteostasis collapse may be among the earliest events that fail during aging.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 2) | PROPERTIES OF PROTEIN FOLDING DISEASES

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 2) | PROPERTIES OF PROTEIN FOLDING DISEASES
items.title: Complexity of Protein Folding Diseases and Aging
items.content: Protein folding diseases are complex due to the risk across all tissues and proteins, with variable onset ages. Stressors like pneumonia or fractures can exacerbate these conditions, leading to rapid accumulation of age-related morbidities.
keyPoints: All tissues and proteins are at risk for misfolding in protein folding diseases.
Age of onset varies widely among different protein folding diseases.
Systemic stress can trigger clinical manifestations after a delay (1–2 years).
Decline in proteostasis network components may lead to multiple morbidities in the elderly.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 2) | PROPERTIES OF PROTEIN FOLDING DISEASES

items.subtopic: DISORDERS THAT ENHANCE CLIENT MISFOLDING AND CAUSE PREMATURE DEGRADATION (CYSTIC FIBROSIS)
items.title: Understanding Cystic Fibrosis as a Protein Folding Disorder
items.content: Cystic fibrosis is primarily caused by mutations in the CFTR gene, leading to misfolding and premature degradation of the CFTR protein. The most common mutation, F508del, results in the protein being recognized as misfolded and targeted for degradation.
keyPoints: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a recessive disorder caused by mutations in both alleles of the CFTR gene.
The most prominent mutation, deletion of phenylalanine 508 (F508del), is present in ~90% of CF patients.
Mutant F508del is recognized as misfolded and marked with ubiquitin for degradation by the UPS.
Combination therapies that protect misfolded CFTR from degradation can improve patient outcomes.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · DISORDERS THAT ENHANCE CLIENT MISFOLDING AND CAUSE PREMATURE DEGRADATION (CYSTIC FIBROSIS) | DISORDERS THAT INDUCE TOXIC AGGREGATES AND LOSS OF FUNCTION IN MULTIPLE TISSUES ( $ \alpha_{1} $-ANTITRYPSIN DEFICIENCY)

items.subtopic: DISORDERS THAT INDUCE TOXIC AGGREGATES AND LOSS OF FUNCTION IN MULTIPLE TISSUES ( $ \alpha_{1} $-ANTITRYPSIN DEFICIENCY)
items.title: Pathophysiology of Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency
items.content: $\alpha_{1}$-Antitrypsin deficiency is a co-dominant inherited disorder that leads to toxic protein aggregates in the liver and loss-of-function in the lungs. The SERPINA1 gene mutations cause misfolding, resulting in liver cirrhosis and emphysema.
keyPoints: $\alpha_{1}$-Antitrypsin deficiency (AATD) is a co-dominant inherited disease.
Mutations in the SERPINA1 gene lead to misfolded $\alpha_{1}$-antitrypsin, causing toxic aggregates and hepatocyte death in the liver.
In the lung, insufficient secretion of $\alpha_{1}$-antitrypsin leads to proteolytic damage, manifesting as emphysema and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · DISORDERS THAT ENHANCE CLIENT MISFOLDING AND CAUSE PREMATURE DEGRADATION (CYSTIC FIBROSIS) | DISORDERS THAT INDUCE TOXIC AGGREGATES AND LOSS OF FUNCTION IN MULTIPLE TISSUES ( $ \alpha_{1} $-ANTITRYPSIN DEFICIENCY)

items.subtopic: ■ INTERACTIONS WITH PN COMPONENTS THAT CHANGE CONFORMATION, STABILITY, OR FUNCTION (CANCER)
items.title: Role of p53 Mutations in Cancer Progression
items.content: Mutations in the tumor suppressor gene p53 are common in cancer and can lead to altered interactions with heat shock protein 70 (HSP70) chaperones. This affects its DNA binding properties, impairing its function as a transcription factor that normally suppresses genes involved in apoptosis resistance.
keyPoints: p53 mutations are among the most common in cancer patients.
Deletion of p53 combined with oncogene overexpression can drive metastatic cancer in mice.
Mutant p53 alters interactions with HSP70 chaperones, affecting its tumor suppressor function.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ■ INTERACTIONS WITH PN COMPONENTS THAT CHANGE CONFORMATION, STABILITY, OR FUNCTION (CANCER) | STRONGLY ENHANCED AGGREGATION PROPENSITY AND AMYLOID FORMATION (ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE, PARKINSON'S DISEASE, AMYOTROPHIC LATERAL SCLEROSIS, HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE, TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS)

items.subtopic: STRONGLY ENHANCED AGGREGATION PROPENSITY AND AMYLOID FORMATION (ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE, PARKINSON'S DISEASE, AMYOTROPHIC LATERAL SCLEROSIS, HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE, TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS)
items.title: Mechanisms of Protein Aggregation in Neurodegenerative Diseases
items.content: Protein aggregation and amyloid formation are central to several neurodegenerative diseases. Mutations or sequence motifs can enhance the propensity for proteins to aggregate into oligomers, protofibrils, and amyloid fibrils, which can spread between cells and cause widespread pathology.
keyPoints: Beta sheet motifs promote aggregation when exposed to cellular environments.
Aggregates form as oligomers (2–24 molecules), protofibrils (4–11 nm wide, 200 nm long), and amyloid fibrils (microns in length).
Mutations accelerate aggregation in diseases like Huntington's, Alzheimer's, and ALS.
Congo red staining detects beta sheets for diagnosing amyloidosis.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ■ INTERACTIONS WITH PN COMPONENTS THAT CHANGE CONFORMATION, STABILITY, OR FUNCTION (CANCER) | STRONGLY ENHANCED AGGREGATION PROPENSITY AND AMYLOID FORMATION (ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE, PARKINSON'S DISEASE, AMYOTROPHIC LATERAL SCLEROSIS, HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE, TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS)

items.subtopic: SECRETED AGGREGATED AND AMYLOID SPECIES CAUSING SYSTEMIC AMYLOIDOSIS
items.title: Understanding Systemic Amyloidosis: Role of Aggregation-Prone Proteins
items.content: Systemic amyloidosis involves the deposition of protein aggregates in tissues, primarily due to secretion of large amounts of aggregation-prone proteins. These include immunoglobulins from plasma cells and transthyretin, which can be exacerbated by mutations enhancing polymerization. The resulting cellular toxicity and inflammation disrupt organ function.
keyPoints: Aggregation-prone proteins such as immunoglobulins and transthyretin contribute to systemic amyloidosis.
Mutations in transthyretin increase the risk of amyloidosis with age.
Protein aggregates cause cellular toxicity, inflammation, and matrix reorganization, leading to organ-specific dysfunction.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · SECRETED AGGREGATED AND AMYLOID SPECIES CAUSING SYSTEMIC AMYLOIDOSIS | NATIVE PROTEINS PRONE TO AGGREGATE WHEN THE CELLULAR ENVIRONMENT IS ALTERED BY STRESS AND AGING | INFECTIOUS DISEASES AND IMBALANCED CELL STRESS RESPONSES IN AGING

items.subtopic: INFECTIOUS DISEASES AND IMBALANCED CELL STRESS RESPONSES IN AGING
items.title: Impact of Aging on Stress Response in Infectious Diseases
items.content: Aging leads to a decline in cell stress responses and proteostasis, increasing morbidity and mortality from infectious diseases like pneumonia. This is evident in the disproportionate impact on elderly individuals during events such as the COVID-19 pandemic. The impaired function of the protein network (PN) may result in seeding of toxic aggregates, causing long-term damage.
keyPoints: Aging diminishes cell stress response robustness and proteostasis.
Pneumonia is a leading cause of death from infectious diseases in older adults.
Impaired PN function during stress can lead to seeding of toxic aggregates with lasting consequences.
Post-pneumonia, elderly patients may experience persistent lung injury, muscle dysfunction, kidney disease, cognitive issues, and increased cardiovascular risk.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · SECRETED AGGREGATED AND AMYLOID SPECIES CAUSING SYSTEMIC AMYLOIDOSIS | NATIVE PROTEINS PRONE TO AGGREGATE WHEN THE CELLULAR ENVIRONMENT IS ALTERED BY STRESS AND AGING | INFECTIOUS DISEASES AND IMBALANCED CELL STRESS RESPONSES IN AGING

items.subtopic: Protein Folding Disorders
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a consequence of protein misfolding as described in the passage?
options: Premature or inhibition of protein clearance
items.explanation: The passage mentions enhanced aggregation, mislocalization, and premature/inhibited clearance as consequences of protein misfolding, but not increased synthesis of molecular chaperones.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · Protein Folding Disorders

items.subtopic: Protein Folding Disorders
items.question: What is a major contributing risk factor for age-associated protein folding disorders?
items.explanation: The passage states that aging is the major contributing risk factor for these diseases.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · Protein Folding Disorders

items.subtopic: Protein Folding Disorders
items.question: What role does the proteostasis network (PN) play in cellular health?
options: It balances protein input with output and ensures functionality.
items.explanation: The passage explains that the PN balances input with output to ensure every protein is functional, thus maintaining cellular health.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · Protein Folding Disorders

items.subtopic: Protein Folding Disorders
items.question: Which of the following diseases is NOT mentioned as an example of a protein folding disorder?
items.explanation: The passage lists Alzheimer's, frontal temporal dementia, and Huntington's as examples but does not mention rheumatoid arthritis.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · Protein Folding Disorders

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following chaperones are involved in co- and posttranslational folding for proteins residing in the cytoplasm and nucleus?
items.explanation: RQC and chaperones like HSP70, HSP90, and HSP60 regulate the folded state of proteins in the cytoplasm and nucleus.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary role of chaperones like HSP70 and J-domain family members during protein folding?
options: They interact with hydrophobic regions to regulate partially folded intermediates.
items.explanation: HSP70 and J-domain family chaperones transiently interact with hydrophobic regions of nascent polypeptides using nucleotide hydrolysis energy to regulate partially folded intermediates.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)
items.question: Which proteins are primarily responsible for disaggregating protein aggregates?
options: ClpB in bacteria, Hsp104 in yeast and plants
items.explanation: Disaggregases like ClpB, Hsp104, and their metazoan counterparts are responsible for unraveling protein aggregates.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)
items.question: What is the fate of proteins that misfold in the endoplasmic reticulum?
options: They are refolded by ER chaperones.
They are retro-translocated to the cytoplasm for degradation.
They are exported out of the cell.
items.explanation: Misfolded proteins in the ER are recognized, retro-translocated to the cytoplasm, and directed to the ubiquitin-proteasome system for degradation.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 2)
items.question: Which pathway is primarily responsible for the recognition and tagging of most proteins for degradation?
items.explanation: The UPS is generally considered the primary pathway for recognizing and tagging most proteins for degradation.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 2)
items.question: What role does ubiquitination play in protein quality control?
options: It prevents the synthesis of new proteins.
It marks proteins for degradation and has additional cellular functions.
It solely targets proteins to lysosomes without involvement of proteasomes.
It enhances the folding efficiency of misfolded proteins.
items.explanation: Ubiquitination tags proteins for degradation and also plays roles in other cellular processes.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 2)
items.question: What is the role of CHIP in protein quality control?
options: It ubiquitinates nonnative aggregated proteins for degradation.
It directly degrades misfolded proteins without tagging.
items.explanation: CHIP ubiquitinates nonnative aggregated clients, aiding in their degradation by the proteasome.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)
items.question: Which molecular chaperone negatively regulates the DNA-binding activity of HSF-1 in unstressed cells?
items.explanation: In unstressed cells, HSF-1 is negatively regulated by the molecular chaperones HSP70 and HDJ-1.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)
items.question: Which transcription factor is activated by IRE1 to induce UPR target genes in the ER?
items.explanation: IRE1 activates XBP1, which induces transcription of UPR target genes.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)
items.question: What is the role of ATF5 in mammalian cells under mitochondrial stress?
options: Regulates transcription of genes encoding mitochondrial chaperones
items.explanation: In mammals, ATF5 regulates the UPRmt by controlling transcription of genes for mitochondrial chaperones.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)
items.question: Which protein is cleaved to activate HRI during mitochondrial stress in mammals?
items.explanation: OMA1 cleaves DELE1, which activates HRI under mitochondrial stress.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 2)
items.question: Which kinase is activated by amino acid deprivation?
items.explanation: GCN2 is activated by amino acid deprivation.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 2)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 2)
items.question: Which factor is negatively regulated by the redox-sensitive ubiquitin ligase KEAP-1?
items.explanation: NRF2 in the cytoplasm is negatively regulated by KEAP-1.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 2)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 2)
items.question: Which protein does ATF4 induce to lower the threshold for apoptosis?
items.explanation: ATF4 induces the expression of Ddit3 (CHOP), which lowers the threshold for apoptosis.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which cellular stress response is regulated by neuronal signaling in C. elegans and reverts to cell-autonomous control when this signaling is impaired?
items.explanation: The HSR, UPR^ER, and UPR^mt are regulated by neuronal signaling in C. elegans; when impaired, the HSR reverts to cell-autonomous control.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What is the effect of mild perturbation of the mitochondrial electron transfer chain (ETC) in C. elegans?
options: Activates UPR^mt independently of ATFS-1 and HSF-1
Has hermetic effects on organismal stress resilience, proteostasis, and longevity
items.explanation: Mild perturbation of the ETC in C. elegans has beneficial effects on organismal stress resilience, proteostasis, and longevity.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: In which tissue does overexpression of active XBP1 in C. elegans activate the UPR^ER?
items.explanation: Overexpression of active XBP1 in pro-opiomelanocortin neurons activates the UPR^ER in the liver.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What is the role of transcellular chaperone signaling between somatic tissues and neurons in C. elegans?
items.explanation: Transcellular chaperone signaling communicates proteotoxic stress signals via PHA-4/FOXA to control systemic expression of HSP90.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 2) | PROPERTIES OF PROTEIN FOLDING DISEASES
items.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as one of the primary hallmarks of the biology of aging in the passage?

items.subtopic: Protein Folding Disorders
items.assertion: Proteostasis failure leads to increased morbidity and mortality in older individuals.
items.reason: Clinicians observe the consequences of proteostasis failure as age-associated clinical problems.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · Protein Folding Disorders

items.subtopic: Protein Folding Disorders
items.assertion: The PN is not essential for tissue shape and function.
items.reason: The PN ensures diverse protein-protein interactions in cell signaling and biosynthetic processes.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the PN is essential for tissue shape and function, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · Protein Folding Disorders

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)
items.assertion: The proteostasis network prevents off-pathway conformational states.
items.reason: It coordinates molecular machines to regulate protein expression and quality control.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)
items.assertion: HSP70 chaperones stabilize fully folded proteins.
items.reason: They interact with hydrophobic regions of nascent polypeptides using nucleotide hydrolysis energy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; HSP70 regulates partially folded intermediates, not fully folded proteins.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · PROTEIN QUALITY CONTROL MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)
items.assertion: HSF-1 is essential for cell stress resilience.
items.reason: HSF-1 regulates genes encoding molecular chaperones and components of the proteostasis network.
items.explanation: Both HSF-1's role in regulating chaperone genes and its importance for stress resilience are true.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)
items.assertion: ATF6 is activated by IRE1 during ER stress.
items.reason: IRE1 activates ATF6 through its RNase activity.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; ATF6 activation involves relocalization to the Golgi and cleavage, not direct activation by IRE1.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · CELL STRESS RESPONSES: SENSORS AND REGULATORS OF PROTEIN DAMAGE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: The age-dependent decline in the HSR can be reversed.
items.reason: By blocking signals from germline stem cells or preventing epigenetic repressive marks.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how the decline in the HSR can be reversed.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: Mild perturbation of the ETC decreases lifespan.

items.explanation: The assertion is false; mild perturbation has beneficial effects on lifespan, not a decrease.
items.reference: Protein Folding Disorders · ORGANISMAL PROTEOSTASIS IN AGING AND DISEASE (part 1)
